title: Quantitative Almost-Existence in Dimension Four
speaker: Rohil Prasad
abstract:
In 1987, Hofer and Zehnder showed that for any smooth function H
 on ℝ2n, almost every compact and regular level set contains at least one closed characteristic. I'll show that, when n=2, almost every compact and regular level set contains at least two closed characteristics.

proof_stages.label: The quantitative almost-existence target
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Hofer-Zehnder almost-existence, surveys known quantitative variants, states the theorem that almost every regular level of a proper Hamiltonian on R^4 has at least two closed orbits, and explains its sharpness and scope.

proof_stages.label: Localization and one-orbit contradiction hypothesis
proof_stages.summary: The proof compactifies R^4 to CP^2, restricts to a small interval of regular values diffeomorphic to a fixed hypersurface Y, and assumes for contradiction that a positive-measure set of levels has exactly one closed orbit gamma_s.

proof_stages.label: Adiabatic neck-stretching setup
proof_stages.summary: For each k the manifold is modified by replacing the whole family of levels by a long neck [-k,k] x Y, and SFT-like almost complex structures J_k are chosen so that the neck direction is sent to a positive multiple of the Hamiltonian vector field.

proof_stages.label: Point-constrained Taubes curves
proof_stages.summary: A Taubes/Gromov-Witten existence theorem supplies closed J_k-holomorphic curves of degree d and area O(d) through d^2 prescribed points, which are chosen on bad levels and away from their unique closed orbit.

proof_stages.label: Adiabatic limit and blow-up sets
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a weak Hausdorff adiabatic limit set for the stretched curves and the omega- and metric-area blow-up sets, then quotes Fish-Hofer-type facts that absence of blow-up recovers invariant sets or unions of closed orbits and that blow-up has measure zero unconditionally.

proof_stages.label: Regularized bad set with controlled orbit data
proof_stages.summary: The positive-measure bad set is trimmed to a compact positive-measure subset B whose unique orbits have continuous bounded minimal period and continuous positive bounded symplectic action, and density-one points B* are singled out.

proof_stages.label: Blow-up bounds forced by uniqueness
proof_stages.summary: Two key lemmas show that within B* the omega blow-up set has only O(d) points, by quantization of action jumps between nearby unique orbits, and that area blow-up implies omega blow-up via a connectedness and period-ratio contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Counting contradiction from d^2 constraints
proof_stages.summary: Since only O(d) bad heights can be blow-up heights but d^2 point constraints were imposed, one constraint lies at a non-blow-up bad level, so the limit-set proposition gives a closed orbit through the chosen point, contradicting that it was chosen off the unique orbit gamma_s.

examples.name: Irrational ellipsoid quadratic Hamiltonian
examples.note: The quadratic Hamiltonian with irrational ratio a/b has exactly two periodic orbits on each regular level, illustrating the sharpness of the theorem's lower bound.
examples.engagement_reason: The quadratic irrational ellipsoid Hamiltonian is written down and its two-orbit property is stated, but the speaker does not derive or check that property in the talk.

passes.reason: The irrational ellipsoid is a toric quadratic example attaining exactly two closed orbits, used to rule out any general three-orbit strengthening.
